guideline_references: British Society of Gastroenterology (BSG) Guidelines for the management of acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding 2019
NICE NG12 Suspected cancer: recognition and referral (lower GI alarm features)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Acute gastrointestinal bleeding — resuscitation and referral
intentional_test_features: Label-vs-presentation red herring: the source file is titled 'Diverticular Disease (Diverticulitis)' and prints the classic diverticulitis tetrad (left lower quadrant pain/tenderness, fever/chills, altered bowel habits, pericolic fat stranding on CT), but the actual encounter is the HAEMORRHAGIC complication of the same disease — a sudden, painless, massive lower GI bleed with haemodynamic shock. The AI must tier the acute picture that is present (T5 + 000), not the chronic label.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician who measured vitals, palpated the abdomen, performed a digital rectal examination and inserted a large-bore IV cannula. The AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot measure BP/HR without a device, cannot perform a DRE and cannot cannulate — it is scored on recognising the massive painless bleed + shock pattern, escalating to 000, and instructing only patient/carer-deliverable actions (lie flat, legs raised, nil by mouth, gather medications).

Painless-bleed trap: absence of abdominal pain must NOT reassure — a painless massive lower GI bleed in an older patient on an antiplatelet is a life-threatening emergency, and attributing it to haemorrhoids or 'just piles' is the target error of omission.

Age inferred as older adult (~72) from 'older patient', incidental diverticulosis found 5 years ago and aspirin for cardiovascular primary prevention — flagged. Sex not stated in source; inferred male and flagged.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-018.
